I don't spend much time scrolling through these platforms, maybe just a few times a month when I check in on family back home or see what's happening in my community, but even in that short time, I notice things that don't seem right.

What I've learned is that fake content spreads because people don't take the time to really think before they share. In my sales work, I deal with facts and details every day, so I apply the same careful approach to social media. When I see something that makes me angry or emotional, I pause. That's my first rule. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion:
You know, when I first came to Kansas City, I didn't understand social media very well. Now that I'm more settled here with my family, I see how important it is to be careful about what you believe online. I don't spend much time scrolling through these platforms, maybe just a few times a month when I check in on family back home or see what's happening in my community, but even in that short time, I notice things that don't seem right.

What I've learned is that fake content spreads because people don't take the time to really think before they share. In my sales work, I deal with facts and details every day, so I apply the same careful approach to social media. When I see something that makes me angry or emotional, I pause. That's my first rule. Strong feelings are exactly what fake content wants to trigger because then people share without thinking. I ask myself simple questions: Do I know where this came from? Have I seen this from a trusted source? Does it match what I already know to be true?

I've also learned to check the source. If something is shared by someone I don't know well, I'm skeptical. If it's from a news organization, I check if it's a real one or something that sounds official but isn't. My husband and I talk about what we see online, and that helps because two people questioning something together is better than one person alone. This is especially important when it comes to health information or religious content, because these things matter deeply to me and my family.

My approach isn't complicated because I believe in being straightforward and honest in everything I do. I'd rather miss some interesting posts than accidentally spread something false to my friends and family. When you work in sales, your reputation matters, and that's true online too. Being the person who shares real information, not fake stuff, means people trust you more. That trust took time to build in my life, and I'm not going to lose it by being careless on social media.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: This persona is a 43-year-old immigrant woman working as a sales associate with primary school education but high conscientiousness (90) and high agreeableness (79). Her low openness (29) suggests preference for familiar, straightforward approaches rather than complex theories. Her high conscientiousness indicates she is detail-oriented, organized, and careful. She spends minimal time on social media, suggesting practical rather than extensive engagement. Her immigrant background and family focus indicate she values community trust and reliability. Her religious beliefs and family orientation suggest she cares about ethical behavior. The response draws on her sales background (understanding facts, reputation, trust), her conscientiousness (verification steps, pausing before sharing), her agreeableness (collaborative approach with husband, concern for community), and her practical nature (simple, actionable strategies rather than technical solutions). Her low extraversion means she prefers thoughtful consideration over impulsive social sharing.
response_parsed.tone: Practical, thoughtful, straightforward, community-focused, detail-oriented, cautious but not paranoid, warm and family-centered, speaking from personal experience rather than expert authority
